introduction.p: Chapter 4 began with a moving point \mathbf r(t), then widened the language of coordinates and surfaces. We learned that the same geometric object can have several descriptions: an equation, a coordinate condition, a level surface, or a parametrization.
The skill is not to memorize one favorite description. The skill is to choose the one that makes the geometry speak clearly.

subsection.title: The chapter in one picture

row.cell: distance and angle in the plane
distance and angles from a center
equal values of a scalar field
a grid drawn on a surface

subsection.p:
A surface like a sphere may appear in many rows of this table: x^2+y^2+z^2=a^2, \rho=a, or
\mathbf r(\theta,\phi) = (a\sin\phi\cos\theta,\, a\sin\phi\sin\theta,\, a\cos\phi).
Each description sees a different part of the same geometry.

subsection.title: Skills: converting coordinates and recognizing surfaces
subsection.p: By the end of this chapter, the following moves should feel familiar.

paragraphs.title: Coordinate conversions
paragraphs.p: The distance formulas are r^2=x^2+y^2 and \rho^2=x^2+y^2+z^2.

paragraphs.title: Surface recognition
paragraphs.p: A missing variable often means a cylinder. For example, x^2+y^2=9 in space is a cylinder, because z is free.
A squared-distance equation usually points toward a sphere or ellipsoid: x^2+y^2+z^2=25 is a sphere, while \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}+z^2=1 is an ellipsoid.
A variable equal to a sum of squares usually gives a paraboloid: z=x^2+4y^2 opens upward, while z=9-x^2-4y^2 opens downward.
Mixed signs create hyperbolic behavior: x^2+y^2-z^2=1 is a hyperboloid of one sheet, while z^2-x^2-y^2=1 is a hyperboloid of two sheets.
A difference of squares in a graph gives a saddle: z=x^2-y^2.

paragraphs.title: Slicing strategy
paragraphs.p: To understand a surface, slice it.

li.p: Fix x=c or y=c for vertical traces.
Recognize the curve in the slice.

statement.p: Identify 4x^2+y^2+z^2=16. Divide by 16: \frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{y^2}{16}+\frac{z^2}{16}=1. This is an ellipsoid. It reaches x=\pm2,\qquad y=\pm4,\qquad z=\pm4. So it is shorter in the x-direction than in the y- and z-directions.

Parametrize the upper cone z=2\sqrt{x^2+y^2}. Use cylindrical coordinates: x=r\cos\theta,\qquad y=r\sin\theta,\qquad z=2r. So
\mathbf r(r,\theta) = (r\cos\theta,r\sin\theta,2r), \qquad r\ge0,\quad 0\le\theta\le2\pi.
If we only want the part below z=6, then 2r\le6, so 0\le r\le3.

exercises.title: A. Parametric curves and polar coordinates

statement.p: Sketch the curve \mathbf r(t)=(2\cos t,2\sin t), \qquad 0\le t\le\pi. State its orientation.
The curve \mathbf r(t)=(\cos 2t,\sin 2t), \qquad 0\le t\le2\pi, traces the unit circle how many times?
Convert the polar point \left(6,\frac{5\pi}{6}\right) to Cartesian coordinates.
Convert the Cartesian point (2,-2) to polar coordinates with r>0 and 0\le\theta<2\pi.
Identify the polar graph r=5.
Convert r=6\sin\theta to a Cartesian equation and identify the curve.
Convert (r,\theta,z)=\left(4,\frac{\pi}{3},2\right) to rectangular coordinates.
Convert (x,y,z)=(-1,\sqrt3,5) to cylindrical coordinates.
Convert the spherical point \left(\rho,\theta,\phi\right) = \left(3,\frac{\pi}{4},\frac{\pi}{3}\right) to rectangular coordinates.
Describe the cylindrical surface r=2.
Describe the spherical surface \rho=4.
Describe the spherical surface \phi=\frac{\pi}{6}.
Identify the surface x^2+y^2=16.
Identify the surface z=x^2+3y^2.
Identify the surface \frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{y^2}{9}+\frac{z^2}{25}=1.
Identify the surface x^2+y^2-z^2=4.
Identify the surface z=x^2-y^2.
The temperature in a room is modeled by T(x,y,z)=20+x^2+y^2+z^2. Describe the level surface T=29.
Identify the surface \mathbf r(u,v)=(u,v,u^2+v^2).
Identify the surface \mathbf r(\theta,z)=(3\cos\theta,3\sin\theta,z).
Find a parametrization of the plane through P=(1,2,3) with direction vectors \mathbf a=(1,0,1), \qquad \mathbf b=(0,2,-1).
Find a parametrization of the sphere x^2+y^2+z^2=9.
For \mathbf r(u,v)=(u,v,uv), describe the two families of grid curves: u=\text{constant} \qquad\text{and}\qquad v=\text{constant}.
For \mathbf r(\theta,z)=(2\cos\theta,2\sin\theta,z), compute \mathbf r_\theta\times\mathbf r_z. Does it point inward or outward?

subsection.title: Project: build a gallery of quadrics
subsection.p: Create a visual gallery of the main quadric surfaces from .

li.p: a hand sketch or computer-generated plot,
one sentence explaining how the signs and coefficients shape it.

subsection.p:
Use at least six of the following:
x^2+y^2=4, z=x^2+y^2, z=x^2-y^2, \dfrac{x^2}{9}+\dfrac{y^2}{4}+z^2=1, x^2+y^2-z^2=1, z^2-x^2-y^2=1, z^2=x^2+y^2, y=x^2.
For the last equation, y=x^2, remember that this is a surface in space, not just a plane parabola. The missing variable z is free.
A good gallery should not look like a list of formulas. It should show how the formulas turn into shapes.

subsection.title: Project: compare several parametrizations of a sphere
subsection.p: The sphere x^2+y^2+z^2=a^2 has many parametrizations. Compare the following three.

paragraphs.title: 2. Upper and lower graph parametrizations
paragraphs.p:
The upper hemisphere is
\mathbf r_2(u,v) = \left(u,v,\sqrt{a^2-u^2-v^2}\right), \qquad u^2+v^2\le a^2.
The lower hemisphere is
\mathbf r_3(u,v) = \left(u,v,-\sqrt{a^2-u^2-v^2}\right), \qquad u^2+v^2\le a^2.

paragraphs.title: 3. Height-angle parametrization
paragraphs.p:
Use height z and angle \theta:
\mathbf r_4(\theta,z) = \left( \sqrt{a^2-z^2}\cos\theta,\, \sqrt{a^2-z^2}\sin\theta,\, z \right),
where 0\le\theta\le2\pi,\qquad -a\le z\le a.

li.p: Which parameter curves look like circles?
Which parameter curves run from pole to pole?
Where does the parametrization repeat points?
Where does the parametrization collapse a whole edge or curve to one point?
Which parametrization would you use to draw latitude and longitude?
Which would you use to describe the sphere as two graphs?

subsection.p: This project is not only about spheres. It teaches a larger lesson: a parametrization is a choice of coordinates on a surface, and different choices reveal different geometry.

subsection.title: A final hook: once curves move, velocity returns
subsection.p: A curve can be described as a set of points, but a parametrized curve remembers how the points are traced. The circle x^2+y^2=1 does not know whether a particle moves clockwise or counterclockwise, quickly or slowly, once or many times. The parametrization \mathbf r(t)=(\cos t,\sin t) does know.

That difference is about to become calculus again. Once the parameter is time, a curve has velocity. Once velocity changes, it has acceleration. The old single-variable idea \text{rate of change} returns, but now the output is a vector and the path may bend through space.
The next chapter begins there: not with a new kind of surface, but with a moving point.
